Anxiety and Anxiety Disorders
What anxiety is, why it happens, and evidence-based ways to manage it
Introduction
Anxiety is a natural human response to stress or perceived danger, and it is something almost everyone
experiences from time to time. This document explains the difference between normal, everyday anxiety and
an anxiety disorder, describes the main types of anxiety disorders, and summarizes evidence-based

an anxiety disorder, describes the main types of anxiety disorders, and summarizes evidence-based
approaches to treatment and self-management. It is written for adults seeking accurate, non-commercial
information about anxiety.
What is Anxiety?
Anxiety is a feeling of fear, dread, and uneasiness. It can cause physical reactions such as sweating,
restlessness, tension, and a rapid heartbeat. Anxiety can be a normal reaction to stress, for example before

a difficult problem at work, a test, or an important decision, and in these situations it can actually help a
person cope by providing a boost of energy or improved focus.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
The Mental Health Foundation (UK) describes anxiety as part of the body’s natural “fight or flight” response,
which helps people react quickly to potential threats. It is normal to feel anxious sometimes, and anxiety is

not necessarily a bad thing: it can spur a person on, help them stay alert, and motivate them to solve
problems. Anxiety becomes a potential problem when it affects a person’s ability to live their life, or when it is
ongoing, intense, hard to control, or out of proportion to the situation.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
For people with an anxiety disorder, the fear is not temporary and can become overwhelming. Anxiety

For people with an anxiety disorder, the fear is not temporary and can become overwhelming. Anxiety
disorders are conditions in which anxiety does not go away and can get worse over time, with symptoms
that interfere with daily activities such as job performance, schoolwork, and relationships.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
Common Types of Anxiety Disorders
●
Generalized anxiety disorder (GAD): persistent, excessive worry about ordinary issues such as health,

money, work, or family, occurring most days for at least six months.
●
Panic disorder: recurrent, unexpected panic attacks, which are sudden episodes of intense fear along
with physical symptoms such as a racing heart, sweating, or shortness of breath.
●
Social anxiety disorder (social phobia): intense, persistent fear of being watched, judged, or embarrassed
in social situations.

●
Specific phobias: an intense, persistent fear of a specific object or situation, such as heights, flying, or
certain animals, that is out of proportion to the actual danger.
●
Agoraphobia: intense fear or anxiety about places or situations where escape or help might be difficult if
panic-like symptoms occur.
●
Separation anxiety disorder: excessive fear or anxiety about being apart from attachment figures.

●
Separation anxiety disorder: excessive fear or anxiety about being apart from attachment figures.
(Source: NIMH, MedlinePlus)
Causes
The exact cause of most anxiety disorders is not fully understood, but research points to a combination of
biological and environmental contributors. Genetics may play a role, since anxiety disorders can run in
families, and differences in brain structure and function have been observed between people with and

without anxiety disorders. Stressful or traumatic life experiences are also recognized contributors.
(Source: NIMH, MedlinePlus)
The Mental Health Foundation (UK) notes that the causes of anxiety are often complex and can involve a
bidirectional relationship with social and environmental factors. Negative life events, such as divorce, abuse,
a stressful work or education environment, or a traumatic event like an accident or assault, can contribute to

anxiety. Financial strain is also strongly associated with anxiety, as is pressure to meet societal or cultural
expectations around appearance, achievement, or success.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
Risk Factors
●
A family history of anxiety disorders or other mental health conditions.
●
Exposure to stressful or traumatic events, including in childhood.
●
Ongoing stress, such as financial strain, relationship difficulties, or a demanding work or study
environment.
●

environment.
●
Certain personality traits, such as a tendency toward negative thinking or difficulty tolerating uncertainty.
●
Co-occurring physical or mental health conditions, including depression and substance use.
●
Younger age: in UK survey data, anxiety levels were highest among adults aged 18 to 34.
(Source: NIMH, MedlinePlus, Mental Health Foundation UK)
Common Symptoms
Anxiety can affect a person physically, mentally, and behaviorally, and symptoms vary from person to

person. Cognitive and emotional symptoms can include irritability, difficulty concentrating, and feeling
restless, worried, or unable to sit still. Behavioral changes can include avoiding places, situations, or
everyday activities that trigger anxiety.

A panic attack is a distinct episode of sudden, intense fear accompanied by physical symptoms; it usually
peaks within ten to twenty minutes. An isolated panic attack is not itself a mental disorder, but frequent,

unexpected panic attacks along with ongoing worry about future attacks may indicate panic disorder.
(Source: NIMH)
Diagnosis
To diagnose an anxiety disorder, a health care provider will ask about symptoms and medical history. A
physical exam and laboratory tests may be used to rule out other health problems that could be causing the
symptoms. If no other health condition explains the symptoms, the provider may conduct, or refer the person
for, a psychological evaluation.
(Source: MedlinePlus)

for, a psychological evaluation.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
For generalized anxiety disorder specifically, a provider will typically ask about the duration, frequency, and
impact of worry; a diagnosis generally requires frequent worry or tension lasting at least six months, even
when there is little or no clear cause, along with associated physical symptoms such as muscle tension or
difficulty sleeping.
(Source: MedlinePlus)

(Source: MedlinePlus)
Evidence-Based Treatment Options
Anxiety disorders are treatable, and most people who receive appropriate treatment see significant
improvement. The main treatments are psychotherapy, medication, or a combination of the two, and the
best approach depends on the specific disorder, its severity, and individual preferences.
(Source: NIMH, MedlinePlus)
●
Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT): a structured talk therapy that teaches people to recognize and

Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT): a structured talk therapy that teaches people to recognize and
change distorted thoughts and behaviors, helping them respond differently to situations that trigger fear
or anxiety.

●
Exposure therapy: a gradual, structured approach in which a person is progressively exposed to a feared
object or situation to reduce avoidance and fear over time.
●
Medications: including certain antidepressants such as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs)
and serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs), as well as other anti-anxiety medicines;

and serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs), as well as other anti-anxiety medicines;
benzodiazepines act quickly but can become less effective and habit-forming with long-term use, so they
are generally prescribed cautiously and for limited periods.
●
Beta blockers: sometimes used to relieve physical symptoms of anxiety, such as a rapid heartbeat or
trembling.
(Source: NIMH, MedlinePlus)

Finding the most effective treatment can take some trial and error, and both psychotherapy and medication
can take time to produce noticeable benefits. Choosing a treatment plan should be done in consultation with
a health care provider or mental health professional, based on individual needs and preferences.
(Source: NIMH)
Healthy Lifestyle Recommendations

(Source: NIMH)
Healthy Lifestyle Recommendations
A healthy lifestyle can help manage anxiety symptoms and support the effectiveness of formal treatment,
although lifestyle changes alone are not usually a substitute for professional care.
(Source: NIMH)
●
Getting regular physical activity, which can reduce anxiety symptoms and improve mood.
●
Getting enough good-quality sleep on a consistent schedule.
●
Reducing caffeine intake, since caffeine can trigger or worsen anxiety symptoms.
●

●
Reducing caffeine intake, since caffeine can trigger or worsen anxiety symptoms.
●
Limiting or avoiding alcohol, since it can worsen anxiety over time despite short-term relief.
●
Eating regular, balanced meals to help keep energy levels stable.
●
Maintaining a strong social support system through friends, family, or support groups.
(Source: NIMH, MedlinePlus)
Self-Help Techniques
In addition to professional treatment, several self-help strategies can help people manage day-to-day

anxiety symptoms.
●
Practicing relaxation techniques, such as deep breathing exercises, which can help calm the body’s
stress response during moments of acute anxiety.
●
Practicing mindfulness, which involves paying attention to the present moment; note that some sources
indicate mindfulness is not recommended specifically for social anxiety.
●
Talking about feelings with a trusted friend, family member, or counselor, since being heard and
understood can reduce distress.

When to Seek Professional Help
It is normal to feel anxious at times, and this does not always mean a person has an anxiety disorder.
However, professional help should be sought if anxiety is ongoing, intense, difficult to control, out of
proportion to the situation, or if it interferes with daily life, work, relationships, or school.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK, MedlinePlus)
●

(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK, MedlinePlus)
●
Anxiety symptoms last a long time, get worse, or interfere with day-to-day life.
●
Physical symptoms, such as chest pain or shortness of breath, are severe or new, since these may also
signal another medical condition requiring urgent evaluation.
●
A person is avoiding important activities, relationships, or responsibilities because of anxiety.
●
A person is using alcohol or drugs to cope with anxiety.

●
A person has thoughts of self-harm or suicide, which requires immediate help.
(Source: NIMH, MedlinePlus, Mental Health Foundation UK)
A primary care provider is a good starting point and can refer a person to a mental health specialist, such as
a psychologist, psychiatrist, or therapist, for further evaluation and treatment.
(Source: MedlinePlus)

(Source: MedlinePlus)
Myths vs Facts
●
Myth: Anxiety is always a sign of a disorder. Fact: Anxiety is a normal and often useful emotion; it
becomes a disorder only when it is persistent, excessive, and interferes with daily functioning.
●
Myth: People with anxiety disorders just need to relax more. Fact: Anxiety disorders are recognized
medical conditions with biological and environmental contributors, and they typically require
evidence-based treatment such as therapy or medication.
●

●
Myth: Anxiety disorders are rare. Fact: Anxiety disorders are the most common form of mental illness in
the United States, and about one third of U.S. adults and adolescents experience an anxiety disorder at
some point in their lives.
●
Myth: Medication is the only effective treatment. Fact: Psychotherapy, particularly cognitive behavioral
therapy, is an effective evidence-based treatment on its own or combined with medication.

Frequently Asked Questions
Q: Is anxiety the same as an anxiety disorder?
A: No. Anxiety is a normal, common emotion that everyone experiences. An anxiety disorder is a
diagnosable mental health condition in which anxiety is persistent, excessive, and interferes with daily life.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
Q: How long does it take for anxiety treatment to work?

(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
Q: How long does it take for anxiety treatment to work?
A: Both psychotherapy and medication can take time to show benefits, and finding the most effective
treatment plan may involve some trial and error in consultation with a health care provider.
(Source: NIMH)
Q: Can lifestyle changes cure an anxiety disorder on their own?
A: Healthy lifestyle choices, such as exercise, good sleep, and reduced caffeine intake, can help reduce

anxiety symptoms and support treatment, but they generally cannot replace evidence-based treatment such
as psychotherapy or medication for a diagnosed anxiety disorder.
(Source: NIMH)
Q: Can children have anxiety disorders?
A: Yes. Anxiety disorders can affect people of all ages, including children, and can interfere with school
performance, family relationships, and social development.
(Source: MedlinePlus)

Key Takeaways
●
Anxiety is a normal human response to stress and becomes a disorder only when it is persistent,
excessive, and disruptive to daily life.
●
There are several distinct anxiety disorders, including generalized anxiety disorder, panic disorder, social
anxiety disorder, phobias, and separation anxiety disorder.

●
Anxiety disorders are common, affecting about one third of U.S. adults and adolescents at some point in
their lives, and are shaped by a mix of genetic, biological, and environmental factors.
●
Effective, evidence-based treatments exist, particularly cognitive behavioral therapy and, when
appropriate, medication.
●
Healthy lifestyle habits and self-help strategies can support treatment but do not usually replace

●

This document provides general educational information based on publicly available guidance from the National Institute of

Mental Health, MedlinePlus, and the Mental Health Foundation (UK). It is not a substitute for personalized medical advice,
diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified health care provider with questions about a specific medical condition.

Depression: Symptoms, Causes, and
Treatment
An evidence-based overview of depressive disorders and how they are managed
Introduction
Depression is one of the most common mental health conditions worldwide. It is more than ordinary sadness
or a temporary low mood; it is a persistent condition that can affect how a person thinks, feels, sleeps, eats,
and functions in daily life. This document explains what depression is, its causes and risk factors, common

symptoms, how it is diagnosed, evidence-based treatment options, and practical guidance on self-care and
when to seek professional help.
What is Depression?
Depression is a mood disorder characterized by a persistent feeling of sadness, hopelessness, or loss of
interest in activities that lasts for at least two weeks and interferes with daily life. It is different from the
normal emotional experience of sadness or grief, which is typically shorter and less impairing. Depression is

extreme and persistent, and it can significantly interfere with a person’s ability to function at work, in
relationships, and in daily activities.
(Source: NIMH)
Not everyone who is depressed experiences the same symptoms; some people experience only a few
symptoms while others experience many, and the severity, frequency, and duration of symptoms vary from
person to person. Depression is not a sign of personal weakness or a character flaw, and a person cannot

simply “snap out of” clinical depression.
(Source: NIMH)
The Mental Health Foundation (UK) describes depression as ranging from mild forms, which can make
everyday tasks feel harder, to severe depression, which can lead to feelings of hopelessness and, in some
cases, suicidal thoughts. Depression is common, and recovery is possible even for people who have
experienced it for a long time.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
Types of Depression
●

experienced it for a long time.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
Types of Depression
●
Major depressive disorder: severe symptoms that interfere with the ability to work, sleep, eat, and enjoy
life, occurring most of the day, nearly every day, for at least two weeks.
●
Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia): depressive symptoms that last for at least two years, though
they may be less severe than major depression.

●
Seasonal affective disorder (SAD): a form of depression related to changes in seasons, most often
beginning in late fall or early winter and improving in spring and summer.
●
Perinatal depression: depression occurring during pregnancy or after childbirth, marked by severe
anxiety, sadness, and fatigue.
●
Depression as part of bipolar disorder: bipolar disorder involves episodes of depression alternating with

episodes of extreme high mood, or mania; it is distinct from depression alone.
(Source: NIMH)
Causes
Depression is caused by a complex combination of biological, psychological, and environmental factors, and
researchers continue to study exactly how these factors interact. Differences in brain structure and function,
genetics, and imbalances in brain chemistry are all thought to play a role.
(Source: NIMH)

genetics, and imbalances in brain chemistry are all thought to play a role.
(Source: NIMH)
The Mental Health Foundation (UK) notes that different factors can lead to depression, including genetics,
physical health problems, difficult childhood experiences, and stressful life events such as unemployment,
the end of a relationship, or experiences of bullying or assault. A combination of factors often contributes to
depression, and sometimes there is no single, obvious cause.

depression, and sometimes there is no single, obvious cause.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
Risk Factors
●
A personal or family history of depression or other mental health conditions.
●
Major life changes, trauma, or chronic stress, such as unemployment, bereavement, or relationship
breakdown.
●
Chronic physical health conditions or chronic pain.
●
Certain medications and substance use.
●
Difficult childhood experiences, including abuse or neglect.
●

●
Difficult childhood experiences, including abuse or neglect.
●
Being female: after puberty, women are approximately twice as likely as men to develop depression.
●
Major hormonal changes, such as during pregnancy, after childbirth, or during perimenopause.
(Source: NIMH)
Common Symptoms
Depression affects people differently, and symptoms can be emotional, cognitive, and physical. To be
diagnosed with depression, a person must have symptoms most of the day, nearly every day, for at least
two weeks, and these symptoms must interfere with day-to-day functioning and cause significant distress.

(Source: NIMH)
●
Persistent sad, anxious, or “empty” mood.
●
Loss of interest or pleasure in hobbies and activities once enjoyed.
●
Feelings of hopelessness, worthlessness, or excessive guilt.
●
Fatigue or decreased energy.
●
Difficulty concentrating, remembering details, or making decisions.
●
Sleeping too much or too little, or waking up too early.
●
Changes in appetite or unplanned weight changes.
●

●
Changes in appetite or unplanned weight changes.
●
Physical aches or pains, headaches, cramps, or digestive problems without a clear physical cause that
do not go away with treatment.
●
Thoughts of death or suicide, or suicide attempts.
(Source: NIMH)
Sadness is only a small part of depression, and some people with depression may not feel sadness at all.
Symptoms can also present as physical problems, such as a racing heart, chest tightness, or digestive

issues, and men in particular are more likely to seek care for physical symptoms than emotional ones, and
may be more likely to use alcohol or drugs as a coping strategy.
(Source: NIMH)
Diagnosis
A health care provider diagnoses depression based on a discussion of symptoms, their duration and impact,
and family history. A provider may ask when symptoms began, how long they have lasted, how often they

occur, and whether they interfere with usual activities. A physical exam and laboratory tests may be used to
rule out other medical conditions, such as thyroid disorders, that can cause similar symptoms.
(Source: NIMH)
To be diagnosed with major depression, symptoms must occur most of the day, nearly every day, for at least
two weeks, and at least one symptom must be a depressed mood or loss of interest in most activities.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
Evidence-Based Treatment Options

(Source: MedlinePlus)
Evidence-Based Treatment Options
Depression, even in its most severe forms, can be treated, and the earlier treatment begins, the more
effective it tends to be. Most adults see an improvement in symptoms with antidepressant medication,
psychotherapy, or a combination of both.
(Source: NIMH)

Depression: Symptoms, Causes, and Treatment
●
Psychotherapy (talk therapy): approaches such as cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) and interpersonal
therapy help people recognize and change troubling emotions, thoughts, and behaviors; for milder
depression, psychotherapy alone is often tried first.
●
Antidepressant medication: medicines that may help improve how the brain uses certain chemicals that

affect mood; for moderate or severe depression, a combination of medication and therapy is often
recommended from the start.
●
Brain stimulation therapies: options such as electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) or repetitive transcranial
magnetic stimulation (rTMS) may be considered if psychotherapy and medication do not sufficiently
reduce symptoms.
●
Light therapy: an evidence-based treatment specifically for seasonal affective disorder.
(Source: NIMH, MedlinePlus)

(Source: NIMH, MedlinePlus)
Choosing a treatment plan should be based on a person’s individual needs, preferences, and medical
situation, in consultation with a health care provider. Finding the most effective treatment can involve some
trial and error, and once treatment begins, most people gradually start to feel better.
(Source: NIMH)
Healthy Lifestyle Recommendations
Alongside professional treatment, certain lifestyle habits can support recovery and help manage symptoms
of depression.

of depression.
(Source: NIMH)
●
Getting physical activity: even about 30 minutes of walking a day can help boost mood.
●
Maintaining a regular bedtime and wake-up time to support consistent, quality sleep.
●
Eating regular, healthy meals.
●
Avoiding alcohol, nicotine, and drugs, including medications not prescribed for the individual.
●
Trying to do things that were previously enjoyable, even when motivation is low, since engaging in these
activities can help improve mood over time.

activities can help improve mood over time.
(Source: NIMH)
Self-Help Techniques
Self-help strategies can complement professional treatment, particularly for milder depression, though they
are not a substitute for treatment when depression is moderate to severe.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
●
Connecting with people: talking to trusted friends or family about how you are feeling can help reduce
isolation.

When to Seek Professional Help
Anyone who shows signs or symptoms of depression that persist or do not go away should talk to a health

care provider. If a person notices signs of depression in someone they know, they should encourage that
person to seek help from a mental health professional.
(Source: NIMH)
●
Symptoms have lasted most of the day, nearly every day, for two weeks or more.
●
Symptoms are interfering with work, school, relationships, or daily responsibilities.
●
There are thoughts of death, self-harm, or suicide.
●
Physical symptoms, such as unexplained pain, persist despite other treatment.

988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline (in the United States) or contact local emergency services immediately. In the

999 or go to the nearest emergency department.

●
Myth: Depression is just feeling sad and will pass on its own. Fact: Depression is a persistent medical
condition, different from ordinary sadness, that usually requires evidence-based treatment to improve.

Myth: Depression only affects mood. Fact: Depression frequently causes physical symptoms as well,

Frequently Asked Questions
Q: How is depression different from normal sadness or grief?
A: Normal sadness and grief are usually connected to a specific event and tend to ease over time.
Depression is extreme and persistent, lasting most of the day for at least two weeks, and it significantly
interferes with a person’s ability to function.
(Source: NIMH)

interferes with a person’s ability to function.
(Source: NIMH)
Q: Can depression be cured?
A: Depression, even in its most severe forms, can be treated, and most people who receive appropriate care
see significant improvement. Some people experience a single episode, while others may have recurring
episodes that require ongoing management.
(Source: NIMH)
Q: Is it normal to have physical symptoms with depression?

(Source: NIMH)
Q: Is it normal to have physical symptoms with depression?
A: Yes. Depression can cause physical symptoms such as headaches, digestive problems, fatigue, and
changes in sleep or appetite, in addition to emotional symptoms.
(Source: NIMH)
Q: How long does depression treatment take to work?
A: It varies by individual and treatment type. Once treatment begins, people should gradually start to feel

better, though finding the most effective treatment plan may take some trial and error.
(Source: NIMH)
Key Takeaways
●
Depression is a persistent mood disorder, not ordinary sadness, and it significantly affects daily
functioning.
●
Depression is caused by a complex mix of biological, psychological, and environmental factors, and it is
not a sign of personal weakness.

Introduction
Emotional wellbeing is a core part of overall mental health. It shapes how people experience daily life,
handle setbacks, and connect with others. This document explains what emotional wellbeing means, the

What is Emotional Wellbeing?

What is Emotional Wellbeing?
Mental health includes emotional, psychological, and social well-being. It affects how people think, feel, and
act as they cope with life, and it helps determine how they handle stress, relate to others, and make choices.
Mental health, including emotional wellbeing, is important at every stage of life, from childhood and
adolescence through adulthood and older age.
(Source: MedlinePlus)

Emotional wellbeing is not the same as constant happiness. Staying positive does not mean never feeling
negative emotions, such as sadness or anger; these emotions are a normal part of life and can actually help
a person process and deal with problems. Emotional wellbeing instead involves finding a balance between
positive and negative emotions and being able to cope effectively with both.
(Source: MedlinePlus)

positive and negative emotions and being able to cope effectively with both.

The Mental Health Foundation (UK) describes mental health as the way people think and feel and their

health, and there are times when a person’s mental health is better than at other times. When a person

enjoys good mental health, they have a sense of purpose and direction, the energy to do the things they
want to do, and the ability to deal with life’s challenges.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)

Building a Positive Outlook
Staying positive is the habit of seeing the good in situations and expecting things to work out well. It does not
mean ignoring negative emotions, but rather finding balance between positive and negative feelings.
Practicing gratitude, meaning being thankful for the good things in life, is one way to build a positive outlook;

Physical and mental health are closely connected. Being physically active reduces feelings of stress and

What mindfulness is, how it works, and evidence on its benefits and limits
Introduction
Mindfulness and meditation practices have become widely used tools for supporting mental health and
managing stress. This document explains what mindfulness and meditation are, how they are thought to
work, what the evidence says about their effectiveness, and practical guidance for practicing them safely.

What is Mindfulness?
Mindfulness is a technique that can be learned to be fully present and engaged in the moment without
judging anything. It can help a person manage their thoughts, feelings, and mental health. Mindfulness
exercises are ways of paying attention to the present moment using techniques such as meditation,
breathing, and yoga. Training helps people become more aware of their thoughts, feelings, and body

breathing, and yoga. Training helps people become more aware of their thoughts, feelings, and body
sensations so that, instead of being overwhelmed, they are better able to manage them.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
Mindfulness is often linked with meditation, but the two are not the same. Meditation is a structured
technique that involves setting aside time to train the mind to focus. Mindfulness means being present in the

moment, and it can happen at any time, whether through a formal practice or through everyday awareness,
such as noticing the sensations of a meal or a walk.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
Meditation involves focusing attention to help a person feel more relaxed. It has been practiced for
thousands of years and includes several different styles, but common elements include focused attention on

the breath, an object, or a set of words; a quiet setting to limit distractions; relaxed breathing; and an open,
non-judgmental attitude toward whatever thoughts arise.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
While mindfulness has roots in Buddhism, a person does not need to be religious or spiritual to practice it.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
How Mindfulness and Meditation Are Thought to Work
Mindfulness works by shifting how a person relates to their thoughts, emotions, and experiences. Instead of
being overwhelmed by them, practice can help a person notice and manage them with greater awareness,

than dwelling on the past or worrying about the future.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
Relaxation and meditation techniques can help the body shift from a stress response, in which the body
releases hormones that raise blood pressure and heart rate, to a relaxation response, which helps the body

relax and lowers blood pressure and heart rate.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
Common Types of Practice
●
Mindfulness meditation: sitting comfortably, focusing on the breath or body sensations, and gently
returning attention to the present moment whenever the mind wanders.
●
Body scan: focusing attention on different parts of the body in sequence, noticing sensations such as
tension, warmth, or relaxation without trying to change them.

●
Deep breathing exercises: slow, deliberate breathing that can be practiced almost anywhere to activate
the body’s relaxation response.
●
Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR): a structured program combining mindfulness meditation
and gentle yoga, typically delivered in a group over about eight weeks.
●
Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT): a structured program that combines mindfulness with

●
Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT): a structured program that combines mindfulness with
cognitive behavioral therapy techniques, designed to help people with recurring depression.
●
Everyday mindfulness: informally bringing awareness to routine activities, such as eating, walking, or
commuting.
(Source: MedlinePlus, Mental Health Foundation UK)
What the Evidence Shows
Mindfulness-based approaches can significantly reduce symptoms of anxiety and depression, according to

research summarized by the Mental Health Foundation (UK). Structured programs such as
mindfulness-based cognitive therapy are recommended by the UK’s National Institute for Health and Care
Excellence for people with recurrent depression who are currently well, as a way to help prevent relapse.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
MedlinePlus notes that relaxation techniques, including meditation, can help the body relax and lower blood

pressure and heart rate, and that practicing meditation may help a person react in a calmer way to emotions
and thoughts, including those that cause stress.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
While research supports benefits for stress, anxiety, and depression symptoms, evidence for some other
uses of mindfulness and meditation is more limited or mixed, and mindfulness is generally considered a

Self-Care for Mental Health
Evidence-based self-care practices and when they are not enough on their own
Introduction
Self-care has become a widely used term, but it has a specific, evidence-based meaning in the context of
mental health. This document explains what self-care means, how it supports mental health, practical
self-care strategies, and, importantly, when self-care is not enough and professional help is needed.
What is Self-Care?

What is Self-Care?
The National Institute of Mental Health defines self-care as taking the time to do things that help a person
live well and improve both their physical health and mental health. Self-care can help manage stress, lower
the risk of illness, and increase energy. Even small acts of self-care in daily life can have a meaningful
impact.
(Source: NIMH)
Mental health includes emotional, psychological, and social well-being. It affects how people think, feel, act,

make choices, and relate to others. Mental health is more than the absence of a mental illness; it is essential
to overall health and quality of life. Self-care can play a role in maintaining mental health, and it can also
help support treatment and recovery for a person who has a mental illness.
(Source: NIMH)
Self-care looks different for everyone, and it is important to find what works and what a person actually

needs and enjoys. It may take some trial and error to discover what works best for a given individual.
(Source: NIMH)
Why Self-Care Matters
Self-care is not a cure for mental illness, but understanding what triggers mild symptoms and which coping
techniques are effective for a specific person can help that person manage their mental health more
effectively over time. Self-care practices support the body and mind in ways that build resilience against

everyday stress and can reduce the risk of symptoms becoming more severe.
(Source: NIMH)

Sleep Hygiene and Mental Health
How sleep affects well-being and evidence-based habits for better rest
Introduction
Sleep is essential for both physical and mental health, yet many people struggle to get enough good-quality
rest. This document explains why sleep matters, how sleep and mental health are connected, common
sleep problems, and evidence-based habits, often called sleep hygiene, that can help improve sleep quality.
What is Sleep Hygiene?

What is Sleep Hygiene?
While sleeping, the brain and body remain active. Sleep is a natural process that helps the body restore
energy, supports learning and memory, and keeps people healthy. During sleep, the brain cycles through
different stages of non-rapid eye movement (non-REM) sleep and rapid eye movement (REM) sleep, during
which brain waves, breathing, heart rate, and body temperature change throughout the night. All stages of
sleep are needed for healthy, restorative rest.

(Source: MedlinePlus)
Sleep needs vary by age, lifestyle, and health, but general guidance suggests most adults need about seven
to eight hours of sleep a night for good health and mental functioning, with some adults needing up to nine
hours. It is a common misconception that adults need less sleep as they age; in fact, older adults often
simply get less sleep or spend less time in deep, restful stages of sleep and wake more easily.

simply get less sleep or spend less time in deep, restful stages of sleep and wake more easily.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
The Mental Health Foundation (UK) describes sleep and mental health as closely related: living with a
mental health condition can affect sleep, and poor sleep can, in turn, affect mental health. Quality sleep
supports balanced emotions, focus, and overall well-being, while poor sleep can contribute to or worsen
conditions such as anxiety and depression.

Common Sleep Problems
There are many different types of sleep disorders, which are conditions that disturb normal sleep patterns.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
●
Insomnia: trouble falling asleep or staying asleep, or both; it can be acute (short-term), often caused by
stress, or chronic, lasting a month or longer.
●
Sleep-related breathing disorders, such as sleep apnea, in which breathing stops for ten seconds or
more during sleep.

●
Sleep-related movement disorders, such as restless leg syndrome, which involves uncomfortable
sensations in the legs and a strong urge to move them.
●
Hypersomnia: extreme daytime sleepiness, including conditions such as narcolepsy.
●
Circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorders: problems falling asleep or waking up at appropriate times, often
related to shift work or jet lag.
●
Parasomnia: unusual behaviors during sleep or while falling asleep or waking up, such as sleepwalking

or talking during sleep.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
Signs that a person may have a sleep disorder include regularly taking more than 30 minutes to fall asleep,
waking up several times with trouble falling back to sleep, waking up too early, feeling sleepy during the day,
or a bed partner noticing loud snoring, gasping, or pauses in breathing during sleep.
(Source: MedlinePlus)

Myths vs Facts
●
Myth: Adults need less sleep as they get older. Fact: There is no strong evidence that older adults need

less sleep; they often simply get less sleep or spend less time in deep, restful stages.
●
Myth: You can "catch up" on lost sleep over the weekend. Fact: Consistent sleep and wake times support
the body’s internal clock better than irregular sleep patterns, including sleeping in on weekends.
●
Myth: Alcohol helps you sleep better. Fact: While alcohol may help some people fall asleep faster, it
disrupts the structure of sleep and can lead to more fragmented, lower-quality rest.

Stress Management
Understanding stress and evidence-based strategies for coping with it
Introduction
Stress is something almost everyone experiences at some point in life. In small amounts, stress can be
helpful, sharpening focus and preparing the body to respond to challenges. In larger or more sustained
amounts, however, stress can affect both physical and mental health. This document explains what stress

is, what causes it, how it shows up in the body and mind, and evidence-based ways to manage it effectively.
What is Stress?
Stress affects both the mind and the body. A little bit of stress is good and can help people perform daily
activities, but too much stress can cause physical and mental health problems. Learning how to cope with
stress can help people feel less overwhelmed and support both mental and physical well-being.
(Source: WHO)

(Source: WHO)
It is natural to feel stressed in challenging situations such as job interviews, exams, unrealistic workloads,
job insecurity, or conflict with family, friends, or colleagues. For many people, stress reduces over time as
the situation improves or as they learn to cope with it emotionally. Stress tends to become more widespread
during events such as major economic crises, disease outbreaks, natural disasters, or community violence.
(Source: WHO)

(Source: WHO)
MedlinePlus describes stress as a normal and healthy reaction to change or a challenge. However, stress
that continues for more than a few weeks can begin to affect health. It is possible to become used to the
symptoms of stress without realizing there is a problem, so it is important to pay attention to how stress is
affecting you.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
Causes
Stress is caused by stressors: situations or demands, internal or external, that trigger the body’s stress

response. Common stressors include family conflict, school or work pressures, relationship difficulties,
financial problems, and health concerns.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
●
Major life events, such as a job loss, divorce, bereavement, or serious illness.

Introduction
Mental health is a core part of overall health and well-being, not simply the absence of a mental disorder.
Mental health influences how a person thinks, feels, and behaves, and it shapes the ability to cope with

everyday stress, build relationships, work productively, and contribute to a community. Mental health exists
on a continuum that ranges from optimal well-being to severe distress, and a person’s position on this
continuum can shift over time depending on individual circumstances, life events, and available support.
(Source: WHO)
This document introduces mental health as a global public health topic, describing what mental health

means, the factors that protect or undermine it, common risk factors, evidence-based approaches to
promotion and treatment, and practical guidance on when to seek professional help. It is intended to serve
as foundational background knowledge for anyone learning about mental health, and it is written for adults
seeking accurate, non-commercial information from recognized public health authorities.
What is Mental Health?

What is Mental Health?
According to the World Health Organization, mental health is a state of mental well-being that enables
people to cope with the stresses of life, realize their abilities, learn and work well, and contribute to their
community. Mental health is more than the absence of mental disorders; it is an integral and essential
component of overall health. The WHO constitution explicitly states that health is a state of complete

physical, mental, and social well-being, not merely the absence of disease or infirmity.
(Source: WHO)
Mental health is experienced differently from person to person, with different degrees of difficulty and
distress, and potentially very different social and clinical outcomes. Mental health conditions include mental
disorders and psychosocial disabilities, as well as other mental states associated with significant distress,

impairment in functioning, or risk of self-harm. A mental disorder is characterized by a clinically significant
disturbance in a person’s cognition, emotional regulation, or behavior, usually associated with distress or
impairment in important areas of functioning.
(Source: WHO)

Determinants: Causes and Contributing Factors
Mental health is shaped by a complex mix of individual, social, and structural factors that interact throughout
a person’s life. At any given time, a diverse set of individual, family, community, and structural factors may
combine to protect mental health or to undermine it. No single factor reliably predicts whether someone will

develop a mental health condition; many people exposed to risk factors never develop a condition, while
others may be affected with no identifiable risk factor at all.
(Source: WHO)
●
Individual biological factors, such as genetics and brain chemistry, which can influence vulnerability.
●
Individual psychological factors, including emotional regulation skills and coping style.
●
Social and environmental factors, such as poverty, violence, inequality, and environmental deprivation.
●

●
Life-stage exposures, with risks occurring during sensitive developmental periods, especially early
childhood, being particularly harmful.
●
Adverse experiences such as harsh parenting, physical punishment, and bullying, which are recognized
as leading risk factors for later mental health conditions.
(Source: WHO)
MedlinePlus similarly identifies biological factors such as genes or brain chemistry as contributors to mental

health, alongside life experiences such as trauma or abuse, and family history of mental health problems.
Difficult situations, such as managing a chronic illness, caring for an ill relative, or facing financial problems,
can wear down a person’s ability to cope and worsen mental health over time. Conversely, positive steps
such as therapy can improve mental health.
(Source: MedlinePlus)

Risk Factors and Protective Factors
Risk factors are conditions or exposures that increase the likelihood of developing a mental health condition,
while protective factors help build resilience and reduce that likelihood. Both types of factors can be found at
the individual, family, community, and structural level, and they can emerge at any stage of life.
(Source: WHO)

Common Symptoms and Warning Signs
Mental health problems can present in many ways, and it can be difficult to know what is a normal emotional
reaction and what may signal a mental health condition. MedlinePlus notes that mental disorders are serious
conditions that can affect thinking, mood, and behavior; they may be occasional or long-lasting, and they
can affect a person’s ability to relate to others and function day to day.

The presence of one or more of these signs does not automatically mean a person has a diagnosable
mental disorder, since occasional distress is a normal part of life. However, when symptoms are persistent,
severe, or interfere with daily functioning, they warrant attention and, where appropriate, professional
evaluation.
(Source: MedlinePlus)

Screening and Diagnosis
A mental health screening is a standard set of questions that a person answers to help a health care
provider check for signs of a mental disorder. The questions typically address mood, thinking, behavior, and
memory, and screening is a way to detect potential mental health conditions early. If a screening suggests

signs of a disorder, further evaluation is usually needed to arrive at a specific diagnosis.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
There is no single medical test that can, by itself, diagnose a mental health disorder. A provider typically
combines a discussion of symptoms and family history with a physical exam and, where relevant, laboratory

tests to rule out physical conditions, such as thyroid disease, that can produce symptoms resembling a
mental health condition. A mental health evaluation by a qualified provider is then used to establish a
specific diagnosis and guide treatment planning.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
How Common Are Mental Health Conditions?
Mental health conditions are widespread around the world. Global data compiled by the World Health

Mental health conditions are widespread around the world. Global data compiled by the World Health
Organization indicate that in 2021, nearly one in seven people worldwide, more than one billion individuals,
were living with a mental disorder, with anxiety disorders and depressive disorders being the most common.
Despite the availability of effective prevention and treatment options, most people with mental disorders do

not have access to effective care; for example, only around three in ten people with psychosis, and only
about one third of people with depression, receive formal mental health care.
(Source: WHO)
In the United States, national survey data compiled by the National Institute of Mental Health show that
mental illness is common: roughly one in five U.S. adults lives with a mental illness in any given year. Mental

illnesses are grouped into two broad categories for surveillance purposes: Any Mental Illness (AMI), which
covers the full range of diagnosable conditions, and Serious Mental Illness (SMI), a smaller and more severe
subset of AMI that substantially interferes with major life activities.
(Source: NIMH)
The Mental Health Foundation (UK) similarly reports that a substantial proportion of the UK population is

affected by mental health problems at some point, and the organization notes that poor mental health is not
inevitable: with the right preventive action at the right time, population-level mental well-being can be
protected long before a crisis develops.
(Source: Mental Health Foundation UK)
Evidence-Based Treatment Options
Effective, evidence-based treatments exist for the great majority of mental health conditions, and many

people who receive appropriate care improve substantially or recover completely. Treatment approaches
are typically tailored to the specific condition, its severity, and individual preferences, and are most effective
when started early.
(Source: MedlinePlus)

When to Seek Professional Help
It is important to recognize when self-help strategies are not enough and professional support is needed.

MedlinePlus advises that if a person thinks they may have a mental health problem, they should get help,
and that a good starting point is to contact a primary care provider, who can help identify appropriate next
steps, including referral to a mental health specialist.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
●
Symptoms that persist for more than two weeks and interfere with work, school, or relationships.
●
Significant changes in sleep, appetite, energy, or concentration that do not improve.

●
Use of alcohol or drugs to cope with difficult emotions.
●
Any thoughts of self-harm or suicide, which require immediate attention.
(Source: MedlinePlus)
If a person, or someone they know, is having thoughts of self-harm or suicide, this is a mental health

emergency and immediate help should be sought right away, including contacting local emergency services
or a crisis line.
(Source: MedlinePlus)

●
Myth: Mental health problems are rare. Fact: Mental health conditions are common; global data indicate
that nearly one in seven people worldwide live with a mental disorder, and about one in five U.S. adults
experiences a mental illness in a given year.

Frequently Asked Questions
Q: What is the difference between mental health and mental illness?
A: Mental health refers to a broader state of emotional, psychological, and social well-being that everyone
has, while a mental illness or mental disorder is a specific, clinically diagnosable condition involving a
significant disturbance in thinking, mood, or behavior. A person can have good mental health while still living

with a diagnosed mental illness that is being well managed.
(Source: WHO, MedlinePlus)
Q: Can mental health change over time?
A: Yes. Mental health is not fixed; it exists on a continuum and can change depending on life circumstances,
stress, physical health, and the support available to a person. Positive steps, such as therapy or healthy
lifestyle changes, can improve mental health over time.
(Source: MedlinePlus)

Q: Are mental health conditions treatable?
A: Yes. Effective, evidence-based treatments, including psychotherapy and medication, exist for most
mental health conditions, and many people who receive treatment recover or see substantial improvement.
